title: Decision on how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the implementation of a court judgment related to European harmonised standards (case 437/2025/MIK)
text:
Background 1. ‘Harmonised standards’ are technical specifications defining requirements for products, production processes, services or test-methods. European harmonised standards are developed by one of the European Standardisation Organisations, that is, the European Committee for Standardization (CEN), the European Committee for Electrotechnical Standardization (CENELEC), and the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI). European standards are aligned as much as possible with international standards adopted by the recognised International Standardisation Organisations. [1] 2. Although harmonised standards are not legally binding as such, products which comply with them may benefit from a ‘presumption of conformity’ with the underlying EU legislation concerning the specific products. The Court of Justice acknowledged that, in practice, compliance with harmonised standards may be the only viable way for economic operators to ensure that their products comply with EU legislation, [2] as coming up with a different way of complying with EU legislation would be too costly. This is why “ harmonised standards form part of EU law ”. [3] 3. Given this legal status of harmonised standards, the Court of Justice considered, in its judgment in case Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission of March 2024, that there is an overriding public interest in disclosure of harmonised standards [4] under the EU legislation on public access to documents (Regulation 1049/2001). [5] This means that the Commission must disclose harmonised standards even if certain exceptions to public access might be applicable, including the exception concerning the protection of the commercial interests [6] of the European Standardisation Organisations. According to the Court of Justice, the principle of the rule of law [7] requires free access to all EU law - including harmonised standards - for all natural or legal persons in the EU. [8] 4. Public access to harmonised standards is currently ensured through web portals set up by CEN and CENELEC, [9] as well as ETSI. [10] The Commission provides public access to some standards upon request. [11] 5. Against this background, the complainant requested public access to all internal assessments of the Commission’s implementation of the judgment Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission.

6. In its initial reply, the Commission identified two documents as falling within the scope of the complainant's request: (1) a note by its Directorate-General for Internal Market, Industry, Entrepreneurship and SMEs (DG GROW) to the Secretariat-General and legal service of the Commission, of 25 March 2024, and (2) a note of the legal service of the Commission to DG GROW, of 15 April 2024. The Commission refused to disclose these documents in their entirety, invoking exceptions to public access under Regulation 1049/2001 concerning the protection of court proceedings and legal advice, [12] and an ongoing decision-making process. [13] 7. The complainant asked the Commission to reconsider its position by submitting a ‘confirmatory application’.

8. In its confirmatory decision, the Commission identified an additional document as falling within the scope of the request, namely an annex to the first document mentioned above, a proposal from CEN and CENELEC. The Commission maintained its refusal to grant access to all three documents, relying on exceptions related to the protection of privacy and integrity of individuals, [14] court proceedings and legal advice, and an ongoing decision-making process.

9. In particular, the Commission considered that the requested documents were closely linked to ongoing court proceedings [15] concerning how the Commission provides public access to harmonised standards and their publication.

10. Dissatisfied with the Commission’s decision, the complainant turned to the Ombudsman. The inquiry 11. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the Commission’s refusal to grant public access to the requested documents. As the complainant did not question the redaction of personal data in the requested documents, the Ombudsman’s inquiry did not cover the Commission’s application of this exception to public access.

12. During the inquiry, the Ombudsman inquiry team inspected the non-redacted versions of the documents at issue and held a meeting with the Commission’s representatives. The Ombudsman also received the complainant’s comments on the meeting report. Arguments presented 13. The complainant emphasised the public interest in having access to harmonised standards. In this context, he considered that obtaining public access to the documents he requested in this case contributes to this overarching goal, given that the documents are likely to reveal how the disclosure of harmonised standards operates in practice. Furthermore, the complainant considered that, following the judgment in case Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission , existing EU rules on European standardisation [16] need to be amended to provide for the publication of harmonised standards, in all EU official languages. He argued that the requested documents are part of the Commission’s preparatory work to amend these rules and, therefore, there exists an overriding public interest in disclosure. [17] 14. During the meeting with the Ombudsman inquiry team, the Commission representatives explained that the Commission needed to find a solution to implement the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission . The documents sought by the complainant concern the Commission’s internal exchanges on the set-up of this solution. Since the judgment, the Commission has received many further requests for public access to harmonised standards, and some have led to legal challenges before the EU Courts. Some parties before the EU Courts consider the Commission’s implementation of the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission to be too narrow , whereas others consider it too broad. In particular, international standard-setting organisations have challenged the Commission’s implementation because they consider that it infringes their copyright and undermines their commercial interests. [18] The Commission added that the outcome of the ongoing court cases will not only impact the solution now in place in relation to the transparency of harmonised standards, but also pending legislative and policy initiatives, such as a modification of the legal framework regarding standards by the Commission’s proposals COM(2025)503 [19] and COM(2025)504 [20] , as well as the ongoing revision of Regulation (EU) 1025/2012 on European standardisation. [21] 15. In view of this, the Commission invoked in its confirmatory decision several exceptions to public access. Specifically, as regards the protection of court proceedings and legal advice, the Commission argued that disclosing documents containing internal legal advice could undermine the Commission’s ability to receive objective and frank legal advice in the future, as well as the capacity of its legal service to represent the Commission in court proceedings and defend the legality of the Commission’s acts. In this context, the Commission emphasised that, although the requested documents were not drafted for the purpose of the currently ongoing court proceedings, they are closely linked to them and contain sensitive legal opinions. Consequently, their disclosure could affect the equality of arms between the parties before the court, giving one party access to the other party’s internal legal advice.

16. As regards the protection of an ongoing decision-making process, the Commission argued that disclosing the documents could undermine its deliberations and affect its capacity to make informed decisions, given that the implementation of the judgment at issue is still subject to internal discussions and negotiations with third parties.

17. Finally, the Commission could not identify an overriding public interest in disclosure of the requested documents. It said that the requested documents contain ‘ preliminary reflections exchanged between the Commission services in the process of developing an approach to implementing the Court ruling in Case C-588/21P, and thus do not concern legal questions arising when legislative initiatives were being prepared or debated, nor would their disclosure increase the transparency and openness of any legislative process .’ The Ombudsman's assessment 18. The inspection of the documents at issue in this inquiry showed that they contain the Commission’s initial assessment of the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission. Specifically, one document contains DG GROW’s proposal to the legal service on how to implement the judgment (with the position of CEN and CENELEC annexed), while the second document is the legal service’s reply to that proposal.

19. The Commission refused access to these documents in their entirety, relying mostly on the need to protect ongoing court proceedings, legal advice and its decision-making process. Protection of court proceedings 20. The court proceedings exception can be applied to documents that were not drawn up in the context of specific court proceedings, but which present a relevant link with such ongoing proceedings. Moreover, to be able to rely on the exception, the documents’ disclosure must seriously compromise the integrity of the court proceedings and the principle of equality of arms. The Court of Justice has stated that this could occur “ if parties were to benefit from privileged access to internal information belonging to the other party and closely connected to the legal aspects of pending [...] proceedings ”. [22] 21. In this regard, the Commission argued that disclosing the documents would impede the Commission’s ability to defend itself in ongoing court proceedings by revealing its internal assessment of a previous judgment that is closely linked.

22. The Ombudsman inquiry team inspected the documents at issue, as well as the Commission’s decisions which are currently challenged before the General Court. Moreover, the Ombudsman considered information about a related preliminary reference pending before the Court of Justice. [23] 23. Based on a comparison of the contents of the requested documents and the subject matter of the ongoing court proceedings which the Commission is seeking to protect, it is not clear to the Ombudsman how the equality of arms and the integrity of the court proceedings would be impeded by a partial disclosure of the requested documents. The inspection showed that the documents largely contain general information to which the parties to the court proceedings and the public, including the complainant, seem already to have access. Specifically, the Commission describes on its website [24] how it is currently granting public access to some harmonised standards following the judgment. The remaining information in the requested documents, which is not publicly available, does not appear to be sensitive from the perspective of the ongoing court proceedings, except for specific passages that could be redacted.

24. Overall, the Ombudsman is not convinced that partial disclosure of the requested documents would undermine the integrity of the ongoing court proceedings and the principle of equality of arms. Protection of legal advice 25. In accordance with EU case-law, the legal advice exception can be applied to refuse public access to certain legal opinions if their disclosure undermines the institution’s ability to give and receive frank, objective and comprehensive legal advice. This risk must be reasonably foreseeable and not purely hypothetical. [25] 26. The Commission relied on this exception to refuse public access to the reply of its legal service to DG GROW. The inspection showed that certain parts of the requested document are either general in nature or have already been implemented by the Commission as part of the abovementioned solution to provide public access to harmonised standards. [26] Specific passages that the Commission could reasonably consider sensitive could be redacted. As with the exception regarding the protection of court proceedings, the Ombudsman is not convinced in this context that granting partial access to the document would undermine the Commission’s ability to give and receive frank, objective and comprehensive legal advice in the future. Protection of an ongoing decision-making process 27. The Commission furthermore invoked the protection of an ongoing decision-making process to refuse access to the three documents. Based on the inquiry, it appears that the Commission is mainly concerned about external pressure that stakeholders might exercise on the Commission if they knew the details of its internal assessment of the judgment.

28. While targeted external pressure may constitute a legitimate ground for refusing access to a document, as the EU Courts have emphasised, the reality of such external pressure must be established with certainty, and it must be demonstrated that there was a reasonably foreseeable risk of the process being substantially affected owing to that external pressure. [27] In this regard, the Ombudsman has repeatedly stated that a reference to a generic risk of ‘external pressure’ cannot in itself justify non-disclosure. [28] While the Commission provided some additional confidential explanations in this regard, the Ombudsman remains unconvinced that partial disclosure would result in such external pressure to the extent that it could undermine the Commission’s ability to implement the court’s judgment concerning the disclosure of European harmonised standards.

29. In view of the above, the Ombudsman is not convinced that the Commission was justified in refusing access to the requested documents in their entirety, based on the above exceptions. There was thus no need for the Ombudsman to assess whether there is an overriding public interest in disclosure of the documents.

30. That said, and as noted above, information about how the Commission is implementing the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission is already in the public domain. [29] In the light of the information already in the public domain and the inspection of the relevant documents, the Ombudsman does not see a useful purpose in continuing her inquiry. The Ombudsman would suggest, however, that the Commission reconsider its position based on her observations put forward in this decision, with a view to still granting partial access to the requested documents. Conclusion Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman closes this case with the following conclusion: The Ombudsman is not convinced by the Commission’s position that partial access to the requested documents was prevented by the need to protect ongoing court proceedings, legal advice and a decision-making process. However, given that information about how the Commission has implemented the judgment in Public.Resource.Org Inc. v Commission is already in the public domain, the Ombudsman considers that no further inquiries are justified in this case. The complainant and the Commission will be informed of this decision . Suggestion for improvement The Commission should reconsider its position on the access request and grant at least partial access to the requested documents. Teresa Anjinho European Ombudsman Strasbourg, 20/02/2026 [1] The International Organization for Standardization (ISO), the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) and the International Telecommunication Union (ITU), for further information, please see: https://single-market-economy.ec.europa.eu/single-market/goods/european-standards_en [2] Judgment of the Court of Justice of 5 March 2024, Case C-588/21 P, Public.Resource.Org Inc v Commission , paragraph 75: https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=283443&pageIndex=0&doclang=en&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=9179521 . [3] Ibid., paragraph 80. [4] Ibid., paragraph 85. [5] Regulation 1049/2001 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 May 2001 regarding public access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, OJ L 145/43, https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2001/1049/oj/eng . [6] Article 4(2) first indent of Regulation 1049/2001. [7] Article 2 of the Treaty on European Union. [8] Case C-588/21 P, Public.Resource.Org Inc v Commission , paragraph 81. [9] https://harmonized.standards.eu/ [10] https://www.etsi.org/standards#Pre-defined%20Collections [11] More information is available at: https://single-market-economy.ec.europa.eu/single-market/goods/european-standards/harmonised-standards/access-documents_en [12] Article 4(2) second indent of Regulation 1049/2001. [13] Article 4(3) first subparagraph of Regulation 1049/2001. [14] Article 4(1)(b) of Regulation 1049/2001. [15] Case T-580/24, Public.Resource.Org and Right to Know v Commission , https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=294985&pageIndex=0&doclang=en&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=848706 ; Case T-581/24, Public.Resource.Org and Right to Know v Commission , https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=294993&pageIndex=0&doclang=EN&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=849030 ; Case T-631/24, International Electrotechnical Commission and ISO v Commission , https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=295451&pageIndex=0&doclang=EN&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=849288 ; Case C-155/24, Nederlandse Voedsel- en Warenautoriteit and Others , https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=287177&pageIndex=0&doclang=EN&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=849607 . [16] Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 October 2012 on European standardisation: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2012/1025/oj/eng [17] The complainant referred to various European Ombudsman cases in this regards: Case 1499/2021/SF, https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/case/en/59957 ; Case 1999/2022/SF, https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/case/en/62762 ; Case OI/4/2023/MIK, https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/case/en/64321 ; Case 253/2023/MIK, https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/case/en/63316 ; Case 1053/2023/MIK, https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/case/en/64113 . [18] Case T-631/24, International Electrotechnical Commission and ISO v Commission , https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf;jsessionid=65686B0AFFD292F3A5FE92149712B169?text=&docid=295451&pageIndex=0&doclang=EN&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=848208 . [19] https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/HIS/?uri=CELEX:52025PC0503&qid=1752077900269 [20] https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/HIS/?uri=celex:52025PC0504 [21] https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32012R1025&qid=1752077956298 [22] Judgement of the Court of Justice 24 January 2024, Veneziana Energia Risorse Idriche Territorio Ambiente Servizi SpA (Veritas) v Commission , T-602/22, paragraph 58, https://curia.europa.eu/juris/liste.jsf?num=T-602/22&language=EN ; judgment of 6 February 2020, Compañía de Tranvías de la Coruña, SA v Commission , T‑485/18, paragraph 42, https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A62018TJ0485_RES . [23] See footnote 15. [24] https://single-market-economy.ec.europa.eu/single-market/goods/european-standards/harmonised-standards/access-documents_en [25] Judgment of the Court of Justice of 1 July 2008, Joined Cases C-39/05 P and C-52/05 P, Kingdom of Sweden and Turco v Commission , paragraph 43, https://curia.europa.eu/juris/document/document.jsf?text=&docid=67058&pageIndex=0&doclang=en&mode=lst&dir=&occ=first&part=1&cid=9232339 . [26] See footnote 11. [27] Judgment of 9 September 2014, MasterCard and Others v Commission , T-516/11, paragraph 71, https://curia.europa.eu/juris/liste.jsf?num=T-516/11&language=EN ; Judgment of 20 September 2016, PAN Europe v Commission , T-51/15, paragraphs 30-33, https://curia.europa.eu/juris/liste.jsf?num=T-51/15&language=EN . [28] See for instance, Decision on how the European Parliament, the Council of the European Union and the European Commission handle requests for public access to legislative documents (OI/4/2023/MIK), paragraphs 47-49, available at: https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/decision/en/196680 . See, also, Decision on how the European Investment Bank discloses environmental and social information on projects prior to decisions on funding (2252/2022/OAM), paragraph 26, available at: https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/decision/en/178093 ; Decision on how the European Border and Coast Guard Agency (Frontex) dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning social media monitoring related to migration routes (344/2023/PVV), paragraph 22: https://www.ombudsman.europa.eu/en/decision/en/192720 . [29] See footnote 11.
summary: The case concerned how the European Commission dealt with a request for public access to documents concerning the implementation of a judgment related to European harmonised standards. In its judgment, the Court of Justice considered these standards, which are developed by European Standardisation Organisations upon the request of the Commission, to form part of EU law, given their significant legal effects. As a result, the Court held that the Commission must make practical arrangements for disclosing these standards to the public. The complainant sought access to the Commission’s internal assessment of the Court’s judgment. The Commission refused access to the documents it identified as falling within the scope of the request, invoking the need to protect legal advice and ongoing court proceedings, as well as its decision-making process. In particular, the Commission argued that its internal assessment is closely linked to several cases still pending before the EU Courts. Based on an inspection of the documents, the Ombudsman found that they largely contain general information to which the public and the parties to the ongoing court proceedings invoked by the Commission may already have access. While the Ombudsman was not convinced by the Commission’s position that partial access to the requested documents could not have been granted, she found that no further inquiries were justified in this case, given that information regarding how the Commission has implemented the judgment is already in the public domain. The Ombudsman suggested, however, that the Commission reconsider its position on the access request in light of her observations, with a view to granting partial access.
